summary: Cathy O'Neil examines Georgia State University's algorithm that identifies at-risk students and connects them with advisers, arguing it represents a positive use of big data. Unlike the 'Weapons of Math Destruction' she typically critiques, this system helps rather than punishes vulnerable populations. The algorithm triggered 51,000 advising meetings and improved graduation rates, particularly for low-income and minority students. O'Neil emphasizes that the real value lies in the human intervention the algorithm enables, not the technology itself. She notes that the same algorithm could be used destructively, as nearly happened at Mount St. Mary's College, illustrating that algorithms are tools whose impact depends on their application.
key_points: A hallmark of destructive algorithms (WMDs) is punishing the poor and marginalized; this algorithm does the opposite by offering help
The algorithm's value is in directing human effort—it prompted 51,000 adviser meetings, 3-4x more than before
Results include 6-point graduation rate increase, faster degree completion saving $12M in tuition, and closing achievement gaps for underrepresented students
The same predictive algorithm could be weaponized to punish struggling students rather than help them
Potential flaws exist (some needy students may be missed), but the worst case is omission of help rather than active harm
money_quotes: One of the hallmark characteristics of a WMD is that it punishes the poor, the unlucky, the sick, or the marginalized. This algorithm does the opposite – it offers them help.
The real 'secret sauce' in this system is the extraordinary amount of advising that's been given to the students. The algorithm just directed that work.
A GPS alert doesn't put a student on academic probation or trigger any automatic consequence. Instead, it's the catalyst for a conversation.
This algorithm, which identifies struggling students and helps them, is an example I often use in explaining that an algorithm is not inherently good or evil.
This same algorithm could be used for evil, to punish the badly off.
Low-income, first-generation and minority students have closed the graduation rate gap.
key_insight: Algorithms are neither inherently good nor evil—the same predictive model can either help struggling students or punish them, depending entirely on how institutions choose to act on its outputs.
